text: The patient is positioned in the Lloyd Davis position, with the surgeon standing between the patient's legs, preparing for the laparoscopic splenectomy.
The surgeon retracts the omentum above the transverse mesocolon, tilting the patient to the right to expose the left colic flexure and its attachments.
The surgeon divides the phrenicocolic ligament, releasing the left colonic flexure and exposing the inferior pole of the spleen.
The surgical field displays the colon, small bowel, stomach, and spleen, with the inferior polar artery and vein clearly visible, as identified in preoperative scans.
The surgeon dissects and takes down the splenicocolic ligament, followed by the preparation of the inferior polar vessels, with the spleen and surrounding structures visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Maryland clamp to perform careful blunt dissection, creating a small window around the inferior polar vessel. Three hemlock clips are applied to secure the vessel as part of the standardized technique.
Two hemlock clips are placed on the remaining side of the inferior polar vein, while one clip is positioned on the specimen side.

The surgeon performs a section and proceeds to dissect the renal splenic ligament, the posterior attachment of the spleen. The dissection is carried out deeply to maximize space, which will aid in the subsequent dissection of the superior pole.
The surgeon focuses on the dissection of the inferior polar artery, carefully navigating the anatomical relations as novel structures are closely situated near the dissection plane.

The surgeon performs dissection and ligation of the inferior polar vessels using blunt dissection with a Maryland clamp to create a small window around the vessel, followed by the application of three hemlock clips.
The surgeon prepares the short gastric vessels and divides the gastrosplenic ligament, maintaining awareness of the proximity to the splenic hilum during the procedure.
The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the gastrosplenic ligament, effectively sealing the short gastric vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon divides part of the phrenicus splenic ligament, which is visible as a connective tissue structure attaching the spleen to the diaphragm.
The surgeon approaches the splenic hilum, where the tortuous vessels are visible, requiring careful handling due to the high risk of bleeding.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the splenic vessels to create a passage, allowing the stapling device to be positioned for stapling the vessels whole.
The surgeon uses a Maryland clamp to facilitate the passage around the vessels, allowing the stapler to pass through and secure the vessels at the splenic hilum.
The surgeon holds the stapler closed for thirty seconds at the splenic hilum to reduce the risk of post-stapler bleeding.

The surgeon divides the remaining gastrosplenic and phrenicosplenic ligaments, freeing the spleen completely. The specimen is then extracted through a midline incision using a wound protector and placed inside an endobag.
